perturbation.original_text: The Information shall be kept confidential in perpetuity, and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement. The Receiving Party shall take all reasonable precautions to prevent the disclosure of the Information to any third party, without the prior written consent of the Delivering Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Information shall be kept confidential for a period of 6 months, and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement. The Receiving Party shall take all reasonable precautions to prevent the disclosure of the Information to any third party, without the prior written consent of the Delivering Party.
perturbation.explanation: California law treats trade secrets differently and protects them indefinitely as long as they remain trade secrets. Limiting confidentiality to 6 months contradicts this protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act provides for the legal protection of trade secrets, which extends indefinitely as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret and is actively protected. Limiting the confidentiality period to 6 months would fail to adequately protect trade secrets under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a legal contradiction because it limits the confidentiality period to 6 months, which is inconsistent with the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) that protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain trade secrets. The original text ensures confidentiality 'in perpetuity,' aligning with the indefinite protection required for trade secrets under CUTSA. The changed text's time limit directly contradicts this indefinite protection. Snippet 1 identifies the relevant law: 'This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act,' confirming the relevance of CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice or communication required or permitted by this Confidentiality Agreement (“Notice”) shall be deemed sufficiently given if it is given in writing addressed as provided in the Notice information on the signature pages, and shall be effective upon the earlier of when it is actually received by the party to whom it is directed, or when the period set forth below expires (whether or not it is actually received):(1) If delivered personally, when it is delivered; (2)If transmitted by facsimile transmission (“Fax”), then, provided the party immediately sends a confirmation copy by first class mail, upon: (i) transmission to the party’ s Fax number set forth below, with the party’s name and address set forth below clearly shown on
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice or communication required or permitted by this Confidentiality Agreement (“Notice”) shall be deemed sufficiently given if it is given in writing addressed as provided in the Notice information on the signature pages, and shall be effective only when it is actually received by the party to whom it is directed, with no exceptions.
perturbation.explanation: California law allows for notices to be considered effective even if not actually received under certain circumstances, such as mailing. This modification removes that possibility.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 specifies that service by mail is effective even if not actually received, extending response times based on mailing distance. The modified text, requiring actual receipt for notice to be effective, contradicts this provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 regarding service by mail. The original text acknowledges that a notice can be effective when received or when a certain period expires, aligning with the law that service by mail is complete upon deposit. The changed text eliminates this possibility, requiring actual receipt for the notice to be effective, which is a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states, "Service is complete at the time of the deposit..." which the modified text directly negates.

perturbation.original_text: E. NON-P AYMENT :Any amount to which an Indemnified Party is entitled hereunder which is not paid by the Indemnifying Party within ten (10) days after written demand from the Indemnified Party (the "Default Date"), shall bear interest at ten percent (10%) per annum, or such lesser amount as shall be required by law, for each day from the Default Date until payment in full thereof (after, as well as before, judgment).
perturbation.changed_text: E. NON-P AYMENT :Any amount to which an Indemnified Party is entitled hereunder which is not paid by the Indemnifying Party within ten (10) days after written demand from the Indemnified Party (the "Default Date"), shall bear interest at twenty-five percent (25%) per annum for each day from the Default Date until payment in full thereof (after, as well as before, judgment).
perturbation.explanation: California law sets limits on the maximum interest rate that can be charged. This modification exceeds that limit, creating a usurious rate.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Constitution, Article XV, Section 1, limits the interest rate for loans and forbearance to the higher of 10% per annum or 5% plus the rate established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco. Charging 25% interest violates this usury law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the interest rate from 10% to 25% per annum. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation indicate that this exceeds the maximum interest rate allowed under California usury laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the California Constitution Article XV, Section 1, states that the interest rate for loans primarily for personal, family, or household purposes should not exceed 10% per annum. For other uses, it should not exceed the higher of 10% per annum or 5% plus the rate established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco. Since the changed text specifies a 25% interest rate, it directly violates the specified limits outlined in the law.

perturbation.original_text: A. CLAIMS: Each of the Seller and the Buyer (“Indemnifying Party”) shall indemnify, defend, with counsel reasonably satisfactory to the other party, and hold the other party free, clear and harmless from and against any and all claims, demands, liens, agreements, contracts, covenants, actions, suits, causes of action, obligations, controversies, debts, fees, penalties, costs, expenses, damages, losses, taxes, fines, injuries, judgments, orders and liabilities, (including, without limitation, damage to property and injuries to or death of any person, reasonable attorneys' fees and expenses, consultants' fees and expenses, expert witness fees and expenses and court fees and costs), of whatsoever character , whether known or unknown, suspected or unsuspected (collectively, "Claims") arising out of or related to the failure by the Indemnifying Party or its Representatives to strictly and fully comply with the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: A. CLAIMS: Each of the Seller and the Buyer (“Indemnifying Party”) shall indemnify, defend, with counsel reasonably satisfactory to the other party, and hold the other party free, clear and harmless from and against any and all claims, demands, liens, agreements, contracts, covenants, actions, suits, causes of action, obligations, controversies, debts, fees, penalties, costs, expenses, damages, losses, taxes, fines, injuries, judgments, orders and liabilities, (including, without limitation, damage to property and injuries to or death of any person, reasonable attorneys' fees and expenses, consultants' fees and expenses, expert witness fees and expenses and court fees and costs), of whatsoever character , whether known or unknown, suspected or unsuspected (collectively, "Claims") arising out of or related to the failure by the Indemnifying Party or its Representatives to strictly and fully comply with the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement, including claims arising from the Indemnified Party's own gross negligence.
perturbation.explanation: California law prohibits indemnity agreements from covering liability arising from the indemnitee's own gross negligence.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2782 generally invalidates indemnity agreements that purport to indemnify a party for their own sole negligence or willful misconduct, and courts have interpreted this to extend to gross negligence as well. Modifying the indemnity clause to include claims arising from the Indemnified Party's own gross negligence is therefore unenforceable under California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2782. (a) Except as provided in Sections 2782.1, 2782.2, 2782.5, and 2782.6, provisions, clauses, covenants, or agreements contained in, collateral to, or affecting any construction contract and that purport to indemnify the promisee against liability for damages for death or bodily injury to persons, injury to property, or any other loss, damage or expense arising from the sole negligence or willful misconduct of the promisee or the promisee’s agents, servants, or independent contractors who are directly responsible to the promisee, or for defects in design furnished by those persons, are against public policy and are void and unenforceable; provided, however, that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers’ compensation, or agreement issued by an admitted insurer as defined by the Insurance Code. (b) (1) Except as provided in Sections 2782.1, 2782.2, and 2782.5, provisions, clauses, covenants, or agreements contained in, collateral to, or affecting any constr
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts California law regarding indemnity agreements. The original text provides a general indemnity clause. The changed text expands this clause to include claims arising from the Indemnified Party's own gross negligence. California Civil Code § 2782(a), as evidenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that agreements that indemnify a party against liability for damages arising from their sole negligence or willful misconduct are against public policy and unenforceable. While the snippet doesn't explicitly mention 'gross negligence,' the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both clarify that courts have interpreted this to extend to gross negligence as well, making the modification a clear violation of the law. The added language directly undermines the legal restriction on indemnifying parties for their own high level of negligence.

perturbation.original_text: If transmitted by electronic mail (“Email”), one (or___) Day(s) After the email was sent provided that a copy of the notice is sent, on the same date, to email #2, the text number, or fax number set forth below;(4)If deposited with the U.S. Mail, post prepaid, as registered or certified mail and addressed to the party to receive it as set forth below, seven (7) days after such deposit;
perturbation.changed_text: If transmitted by electronic mail (“Email”), seven (or___) days After the email was sent provided that a copy of the notice is sent, on the same date, to email #2, the text number, or fax number set forth below;(4)If deposited with the U.S. Mail, post prepaid, as registered or certified mail and addressed to the party to receive it as set forth below, one (1) days after such deposit;
perturbation.explanation: California law states that mailing something adds 5 days to the notice period, which this modification removes, additionally, emails, under the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act have different provisions, that are also violated.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 and the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 states that service by mail is effective even if not actually received, extending response times based on mailing distance. By shortening the mailing notice period from 7 days to 1 day this violates the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the notice period for both email and mail, directly contradicting California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013. The original text specifies one day for email and seven days for mail. The changed text alters these to seven days for email and one day for mail, respectively. CCP 1013(a) explicitly states that service by mail extends the response time by five calendar days if the address and mailing location are within California. By shortening the mailing notice period to one day, the changed text violates this provision. The perturbation explanation also points to a violation of the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act, although the snippet does not explicitly address electronic transactions. The law snippet clearly indicates the extension required for mail service, which the modified contract text does not adhere to.
